Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours, but then I'd just end up scrolling through my phone or watching random videos. When I did sit down, I preferred visual stuff like diagrams and videos over reading walls of text. Most weeks I'd put in some hours, but it was pretty scattered and unfocused.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd maybe study more when deadlines were breathing down my neck, but even then it wasn't consistent. Some weeks I'd do nothing, other weeks I'd cram. It really depended on what else was going on in my life and whether I could muster up the energy to care.
Q3: Usually a deadline or some notification about an assignment would make me log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent, scroll around for a bit, and then leave without doing much. It was more about checking boxes than actually wanting to learn something.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. I never got into a groove where logging in felt natural or automatic. It always felt like a chore I was putting off. Even when I tried to make it a habit, something else would distract me or I'd just lose interest after a few minutes.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I'm a visual learner and I could watch them at my own pace. Sometimes I'd rewind parts that didn't make sense. The gamified tasks they introduced were kind of interesting at first, but the novelty wore off pretty quick for me.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and discussion forums. The readings were long and boring, and I couldn't stay focused on them. The forums felt like a waste of time because nobody really said anything meaningful. I'd skim them occasionally but never really engaged.
Q7: If something felt quick and had some visual element, I'd be more likely to do it. Activities that felt like they'd take forever or required a lot of reading just made me zone out. I needed something that grabbed my attention right away or I'd lose interest.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it. Required activities at least had some consequence attached, so I'd force myself through them. Optional ones just felt like extra work with no real payoff.
Q9: Sometimes I'd download a video or PDF and then never look at it again on the LMS. I'd have it saved on my laptop thinking I'd study it later. Whether I actually did is another story. I also used YouTube sometimes to find explanations that made more sense to me.
Q10: There were times I'd stare at my notes or a downloaded lecture for a while, trying to understand something. That wouldn't show up anywhere in the system. Or I'd think about course concepts while doing other stuff, but that's not exactly trackable either.
Q11: Yeah, I'd download videos and watch them offline when my internet was acting up or when I didn't feel like logging in. Sometimes I'd access something once, save it, and then try to study it later without going back online. The LMS wouldn't know about any of that.
Q12: Around midterms everything just felt overwhelming. I had other things going on, money stress, work stuff, and the course material was piling up. I'd look at the assignments and just feel this wave of boredom and dread. It was easier to just not think about it.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or stressed about money, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and watch another lecture. Competing responsibilities always won. If I had to choose between working a shift or studying, work came first because I needed the money.
Q14: All the time. I'd open a video and let it play while I was doing something else, or I'd scroll through materials without actually absorbing anything. Physically I was there, but mentally I was checked out. It was just going through the motions.
Q15: I avoided assignments constantly even when I knew they mattered. I'd tell myself I'd do them tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd push it again. Part of it was boredom, part of it was feeling like I was already too far behind to catch up.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me do anything. Without a deadline, I wouldn't touch the material. Even with deadlines, I'd wait until the last possible moment. It was stressful but I couldn't seem to change the pattern.
Q17: Feedback didn't really motivate me much. By the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. Sometimes good feedback would make me feel okay for a moment, but it didn't change how I approached the next assignment. Bad feedback just made me want to avoid the course more.
Q18: The structure felt pretty rigid and didn't really work for how I learn. Everything was laid out the same way, and it got monotonous. The gamified elements were a nice try, but they didn't fundamentally change how boring the core content felt to me.
Q19: Having so many online courses at once made it harder to focus on any single one. The design wasn't bad, but it wasn't exciting either. More interactive or visual elements might have helped keep my attention longer instead of losing me after a few minutes.
Q20: That sounds like me honestly. You can click around a lot, watch videos, check things out, but if you're not actually processing it or doing the work, nothing gets submitted. High activity doesn't mean high engagement. Sometimes you're just killing time.
Q21: I guess some people are just efficient or study offline. That wasn't really me though. My low activity matched my low output. If someone shows low LMS activity but does well, they probably found better ways to learn outside the system.
Q22: Logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked, how long you spent. But they can't show if you were actually paying attention or just had the tab open. They miss the mental stuff, whether you're stressed, bored, or actually learning anything.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in real effort. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials. For me, I was rarely engaged this semester. I was present sometimes but my mind was usually elsewhere.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how bored and disconnected I felt most of the time. It wouldn't capture the financial stress or the fact that I was juggling too many courses. It wouldn't show that I wanted to do better but couldn't find the motivation to actually follow through.
